The ratio is still below the median. Here is an excerpt from their recent 10-K filing explaining the importance of the North America segment, of which the U.S. is the main contributor to operating performance.

“Given its maturity and role as the primary source of operating cash flow, reduced performance in North America could limit our ability to fund international expansion, strategic initiatives, and shareholder returns”

Profitability

Please take a look at Exhibit 4 for Starbucks’ margin trends from 2016 to 2025.

Let us take a closer look at comparable recent quarter margins. Please see Exhibit 5.

Starbucks turnaround plan is not yet taking hold in obvious areas of the business. Let us again zoom in to the North American segment for Q1 information.

Next we turn to a measure of leverage.

Leverage

Please see Exhibit 7.

Starbucks leverage ratio has increased. Our interpretation of the leverage ratio is long-term debt plus equity market capitalization divided by equity market capitalization.

S&P has a BBB+ issuer rating with a negative outlook outstanding. Moody’s has a Baa1 issuer rating and has remarked that Starbucks in on watch for a possible downgrade. Fitch withdrew their ratings of Starbucks in 2022 for commercial reasons.

Return on Equity (ROE)

For equity holders, the return on equity is an important measure of how much value the business can create. With the DuPont framework, the return on equity can be estimated as the product of the total asset turnover, the net profit margin, and the leverage ratio which we have covered. Please see Exhibit 8 for the return on equity derived from these three ratios.

It is early days for the Back to Starbucks plan to gauge the effectiveness of the plan. Having said that, there is clearly more work to be done. The ROE is well below the median value of 15.2%, and, in the recent Investor Day, Starbucks indicated eventually reaching a payout ratio of 50%. This would suggest a sustainable growth rate of 7.6%.

What valuation would you give a business that has a sustainable growth rate of 7.6%?

Let us take a closer look at the trailing P/E ratio of Starbucks over time. Please see Exhibit 9.

Starbucks currently trades at a forward P/E* of 41.56x and a trailing P/E of 47.93x against a median value of 29.65x from the exhibit.

Source: Seeking Alpha. The P/E multiples used from Seeking Alpha are Non-GAAP

In Exhibit 10 we show the P/E ratio and the ROE for Starbucks.

Risks

Given that we have a sell recommendation, risks to our thesis imply that the operating performance of Starbucks improves and that the market further rewards any improvement in operating performance with a higher valuation. While we are skeptical of the second part of the statement, we do acknowledge that improvements in Starbucks operating performance is plausible. Here are some Back to Starbucks initiatives we believe could improve their operating performance.

For Starbucks, over 50% of U.S. revenue is booked by 11 a.m. which means any improvements in throughput could be impactful. Starbucks has already seen improvements in their target of getting their orders fulfilled inside of four minutes and plans to introduce the Mastrena 3 in 2027. The Mastrena 3 is expected to cut in half the current 70 seconds it takes to pull four shots of espresso while doubling the capacity.

Secondly, Starbucks has improved their mobile app and is enhancing their rewards program, which could also be important drivers of incremental business for Starbucks. Beginning in March, Starbucks will introduce three tiers of membership to their rewards program, which is intended to increase frequency and incentivize customers to reach higher membership tiers. If half of active rewards members transact just one additional time per year, Starbucks has said that this would lead to an increase of $150 million to their top line. In Q1 2026, Starbucks reached an all-time high of over 35 million 90-day active members. Also, in Q1, rewards and non-rewards transactions both grew for the first time in nearly four years.

A third area where meaningful upside could buoy the stock is their international expansion. Starbucks expects to grow coffeehouses internationally at double the rate of North America. If they continue to employ an asset-light, licensed store model for their international expansion, operating margins will expand because of the contribution to the margin from the faster-growing, licensed store model.

Final Thoughts

While we acknowledge that the Back to Starbucks plan may gather momentum in the next several quarters, we believe there is just too much optimism baked in the current price and not enough margin of safety. Taking the totality of the data, including our estimate of the sustainable growth rate, we don't think a premium valuation is warranted. Investors should take advantage of the current price and consider selling their shares.
